title: Cirrus: Block CNI use of google VPCs
body:
Specifically pertaining to executing tests in google cloud, there are
default, pre-allocated class-a subnetworks for each region (data-center).
Each includes a gateway using a `.1` LSB and all are routable from other
regions in google cloud via these gateways.

Because the default CNI configuration also utilizes class-a subnetworks,
this creates the possibility for IPv4 address-space clashes.  Since the
default regional cloud subnets are pre-defined/known, preventing clashes
can be accomplished by seeding these subnets in a dummy CNI configuration.

The default behavior of podman is to grab the highest priority CNI
configuration.  Name the dummy config. appropriate so it always loads
last.  Also name the bridge itself with an obvious name `do-not-use`,
such that any related testing errors should be easier to debug.

Also:

* Minor cleanup of `install_test_configs()`
* Move install_test_configs in `setup_environment.sh` to after possible
  run of `remove_packaged_podman_files()` because that also strips out
  `/etc/cni/net.d/87-podman-bridge.conflist`.

Signed-off-by: Chris Evich <cevich@redhat.com>

comments.body:
@mheon @baude @edsantiago PTAL...sorry I don't have any evidence this has actually caused any problems yet, and I also have no idea what those problems could look like.  I'm also unsure of how to (of if I should) test this in some way other than manually:

```
[root@cevich-fedora-30-libpod-5751722641719296 ~]# make install PREFIX=/usr
...
[root@cevich-fedora-30-libpod-5751722641719296 ~]# podman network ls
NAME         VERSION   PLUGINS
podman       0.4.0     bridge,portmap,firewall
do-not-use   0.4.0     bridge
[root@cevich-fedora-30-libpod-5751722641719296 ~]# podman run -it --rm --name foobar alpine sh
/ # [root@cevich-fedora-30-libpod-5751722641719296 ~]# 
[root@cevich-fedora-30-libpod-5751722641719296 ~]# podman run -it --rm --name foobar --ip 10.128.0.253 alpine sh
ERRO[0000] Error adding network: failed to allocate all requested IPs: 10.128.0.253 
ERRO[0000] Error while adding pod to CNI network "podman": failed to allocate all requested IPs: 10.128.0.253 
Error: error configuring network namespace for container 519391ac0e7f4494d46285aa66cec5756e4eb1d21cce1e063619c7a36d90db3c: failed to allocate all requested IPs: 10.128.0.253
[root@cevich-fedora-30-libpod-5751722641719296 ~]# podman run -it --rm --name foobar --ip 10.170.0.2 alpine sh
ERRO[0000] Error adding network: failed to allocate all requested IPs: 10.170.0.2 
ERRO[0000] Error while adding pod to CNI network "podman": failed to allocate all requested IPs: 10.170.0.2 
Error: error configuring network namespace for container fc0e4e99eafde5ebd3d46f8a447cc1a2b65e03894eebd9a203690d4d720d828c: failed to allocate all requested IPs: 10.170.0.2
```

Best idea I have is to confirm (after `setup_environment.sh` runs) that 'do-not-use' shows up last in the output of 'podman network ls' when `$CIRRUS_CI == true`.
oh...fixing...whitespace error and adding notes about the other changes here...
/approve

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *cevich*, *mheon* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Flibpod). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [mheon] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

Some (hopefully) clarification (if this PR is not merged):

1) Automation creates VM in `us-central` google region with subnet of `10.128.0.0/20`, meaning the VM gets one of those addresses.
2) tests run, CNI (correctly) avoids assigning any address in the `10.128.0.0/20` subnet.
3) random networking test CNI creates/assigns address in `10.170.0.0/20` subnet to a container
4) same or different test fails when packets never arrive - they were routed to japan by the VM's gateway.
I'm really uncomfortable with this hardcoding approach. Did you see if there's any way to get CNI to default to using the other RFC1918 subnets instead?
force-pushed with file-name change for sorting, and to help debugging.

> I'm really uncomfortable with this hardcoding approach.

I'm not thrilled either.  It is possible to define arbitrary networks in google.  Literally ANY private network range can be set.
I'd also add that there is a cloud-API call for retrieving these networks, but having used in elsewhere, it's quite slow (minutes to retrieve them all).
Force-pushed fix to rootless test breakage, added comment, and set filename with '99' prefix.

> Add comment pointing to [VPC network overview](https://cloud.google.com/vpc/docs/vpc#ip-ranges)

...which states:

    unused portions of 10.128.0.0/9 are reserved for future GCP use.

Does anyone know if it's possible to tell CNI to use the RFC1918 class B/C blocks?

Use in what sense? All CNI networks are either the default we ship, or extra networks added by `podman network create` (which isn't yet merged), or extra networks manually added by users. We control the IP allocations for two of those.

> Does anyone know if it's possible to tell CNI to use the RFC1918 class B/C blocks?

@edsantiago yes we can, but...the default we ship is for `10.88.0.0/16`, which may have special meaning w/in OpenShift(?).  I'm not keen on testing with non-default config, but could possibly be convinced if that's viewed as a simpler solution.  Also, @baude's  `network create` (and related tests) interact with CNI to create additional networks, which could potentially also clash(?).

Thinking of other alternatives:  I could delete the default VPC in google and replace _it_ with a class-b or c address range.  (Then do the same thing 5 more times for the other google projects used for containers testing).  This _will_ break in-flight CI jobs, so I'd have to do it at a safe time.

I'm just hopelessly confused. If I can try one last time, why is there a huge hardcoded list instead of just one simple clean `10.128.0.0/9` ? That doesn't collide with the podman default either, and seems much easier to maintain and understand, not to mention more robust.

> why is there a huge hardcoded list 

They're automatically created by google.  The `hack/get_ci_vm.sh` script supports multiple zones, so engineers outside the US can make VMs closer to them (like Valentine).  We have to have one subnet per region to support this, and running automation elsewhere.

It's possible to delete the default VPC networks in google, and then add custom ones of a lesser class for each region.  However, that makes an otherwise auomatic/default setup into manual setup.  Then I need to also do it for all the other `containers/*` repos, to prevent clashes there.  This seems a waste of time when the list of default subnets is known and documented.

Also,  once Brent's PR merges, it will be possible for additional arbitrary subnets (of any class) to be added and used for testing.  That's why I think simply hard-coding the CNI config with the set of known 'don't use these' subnets is easier.  It only affects CI running in google, and positively prevents CNI from possible clash/overlap.  No brain-cells-required on behalf of libpod engineers, they can trust in errors being raised if there's ever a problem.

@edsantiago waitwaitwait, now I understand what you're suggesting `10.128.0.0/20` -> `10.128.0.0/9` and drop the rest in the config.  Sorry I missed that detail, you know, I'm an engineer, I leave details up to users and management :smile:
Updated w/ Ed's suggestion, and added a comment.  I did not check the math on the `/9` suffix, trusting whomever wrote the docs already did that.

Thank you! That looks much better, and eliminates my headache.

>something like this would have been more clear: "Can we simplify the big list down to just 10.128.0.0/9, since it covers the same range, and is documented as reserved?"

The thing is, it _doesn't_ cover the same range. `10.128.0.0/9` is vastly broader than the sum of the set of the listed `/20`s:
```
10.160.0.0/20 -> 10.160.0.0-10.160.15.255
                 10.160.16.0-10.161.255.255 = not explicitly reserved by the list you had
10.162.0.0/20 -> 10.162.0.0-10.162.15.255
...etc...
10.164.0.0/20 -> 10.164.0.0-10.164.15.255
10.166.0.0/20 -> 10.166.0.0-10.166.15.255
10.168.0.0/20 -> 10.168.0.0-10.168.15.255
```
and that was (part of) my confusion: I wondered if there was an important reason for leaving those gaps.

LGTM, thank you.
/lgtm
